body:
My main reason for not doing this is that the implementation is purely based on OpenXR, there really isn't a way to abstract things to some sort of template class that makes a lot of sense and especially for the vulkan extension, we'd be implementing far more on the dummy class while it is never even instantiated (the whole point is that if OpenXR is not available or not running, there will never be an instance).

This really is uniquely OpenXR and frankly frankenstein enough, while I understand why OpenXR went down this path, I really don't think they've architectures this very nicely. From a principled view they way OpenXR sets its hooks into Vulkan only makes sense for a purpose build XR engine.

body:
```suggestion
				Returns the 2D analog of the cross product for this vector and [code]with[/code].
				This is the signed area of the parallelogram formed by the two vectors. If the second vector is clockwise from the first vector, then the cross product is the positive area. If counter-clockwise, the cross product is the negative area.
```

I'm not sure that the `[b]Note:[/b]` part is helpful. Do Godot users care about how something is originally defined by mathematicians for usage in 3D, or do they care about how it's defined in 2D and in Godot? We are already saying that Vector2's cross method is the closest analog to the cross product.

The cross product in 2D is an extension of the original 3D definition, losing the property of the resulting vector being perpendicular to both input vectors (which is impossible in 2D unless the two vectors are colinear but then the cross product is zero anyway) (plus the result isn't a vector). It's the closest analog that exists.

We could keep both explanations if it's helpful for people to understand.

We could also note for Vector3 that the magnitude of the cross product vector is the area of the parallelogram formed by the two vectors. For 3D, we don't have to worry about signed area because that's just indicated by which direction the output vector points.

body:
Ah yes, I'm expecting some discussion on the epsilon, and will need a bit of testing. I saw `CMP_EPSILON2` but wanted to make sure it is ok for this use with 32 bit `real_t`. But you are correct we are using squares here.

It has to be a value that is large enough that when `sqrt`ed afterwards, it still provides accuracy when used as the divisor. So I think a bit of testing will be good to check this in practice rather than going straight for the smaller `CMP_EPSILON2` (without knowing how it was tested).
author: lawnjelly

body: I'm fully agnostic and the only difference with 64 bit would be the lower limit on magnitude
author: AThousandShips

body:
32 / 64 bit epsilon differences can be quite difficult.

Last time we discussed it (that I remember), reduz was in favour of keeping the 32 bit epsilons when compiling as 64 bit. This loses out on some potential accuracy in 64 bit, but if you use different epsilons you open up a massive surface of difficult to find bugs. Epsilon bugs are some of the _worst_ imo. :grin: 

Anyway I'm doing some empirical testing on the epsilon as we speak. :+1: 
Ogre looks like it uses `1e-8f`, which is larger than `CMP_EPSILON2` (which is `1e-10f`).
author: lawnjelly

body:
> Epsilon bugs are some of the worst imo.

Agreed
author: AThousandShips

body:
Ok after (I hope) some testing, I'm satisfied that `CMP_EPSILON2` should be okay at 32 bit, so I've pushed a version with this epsilon.

I tested in gdscript then in a c++ testbed. Of course there's always the chance I messed up, it's pretty easy to mess up, with math precision. :grin:

body:
This is what exactly happens in the reproduction project. Before reaching this part of the code (in _parse_expression > case GDScriptParser::Node::IDENTIFIER), it will go first to GDScriptCompiler::_gdtype_from_datatype > GDScriptCache::get_shallow_script, where it calls `script->set_path(p_path, true);` which push the uncompiled script inside the resource loader.
Another working solution is to change `get_shallow_script` to `get_full_script`, but it might have performance impact.
author: Blackiris

body:
Ah, I just didn't realize that `Resource::set_path()` was changing the cache, I wrongly assumed it only changed a property. So the proper fix would be to move this call from `get_shallow_script()` to `get_full_script()`.

Then there's also the issue with builtin scripts, since they don't really have a path, so there needs to be a way to index them in the GDScript cache so the `finish_compiling()` call can properly reference them.

This issue isn't simple to solve.

body: Importantly, cubic interpolation avoids discontinuities in the 2nd order derivative of the noise function. This is important when using noise as a bumpmap. Regular value noise will show sharp discontinuities when used as a normal map that cubic interpolation avoids
author: clayjohn

body: An important distinguishing feature of simplex noise over other gradient noise types is that the gradients exist in a simplex lattice rather than a grid lattice. This helps avoid directional artifacts that perlin noise suffers from. It also means that it scales better with an increase in dimensions (once you hit 4 or more dimensions simplex noise becomes much faster than perlin)
author: clayjohn

body:
We had a lengthy discussion about this in today's PR review meeting. The consensus was to merge this for now and then evaluate user feedback during the alphas and betas. With the new noise abstract class, it seems like FastNoise would be a great fit for a first-class GDExtension, but the GDExtension system is still relatively new, and we want to get this in the engine and get user feedback now.

Depending on user feedback, (i.e. if users find the current options too complicated) we are considering exposing a simpler type of noise only by default and then exposing the full range of noise types and parameters in an official GDExtension. 

To merge this, the documentation needs to be finished.

One other consideration is ``get_noise_4d()`` I don't like taking away a function that was available in 3.x. Is there any way that we can implement something on top of FastNoise that exposes ``get_noise_4d()``?
author: clayjohn

body:
None of the `get_noise` functions are constant, that looks like an oversight. They can be, see my implementation https://github.com/Zylann/godot_voxel/blob/4c3777ce00473efb426362b83d3c3d5853a2ced0/util/noise/fast_noise_lite.h#L101
Same for `get_image` functions
author: Zylann

body: Does pure virtual means extensions cannot define new noises?
author: Zylann

body:
`virtual` means referencing a `Noise*` that points to a `FastNoiseLite` object will call the FastNoiseLite implementation in the derived class rather than the implementation in `Noise` without casting.

`= 0` means there is no implementation, thus one cannot instantiate Noise().

I don't know about GDExtensions, but when I wrote this as a module this was exactly so I could define OpenSimplexNoise, FastNoise, and FastNoiseSIMD objects, and an unknown number of other types in the future.
author: TokisanGames

body:
What was the idea behind converting this and all of these functions to a template? What other types will be used here? This alpha_blend function is specifically designed for a 32-bit long int, the `l` component of the l2c union. I think there will be unexpected results with other types.

Are there any active use cases right now for generating non-32-bit noise images? Were they tested and do they work, including with the alpha blend seamless function?
author: TokisanGames

body:
I think the purpose of this was to allow for grayscale image optimizations when a noise generator creates an image with `Image::FORMAT_L8` (see the template specialization and the get_seamless_image implementation in noise.cpp). 
But looking at the code now I think there is room for improvement, which I might include in a subsequent PR. E.g. I can't remember why I didn't just overload the alpha blend function.

body: I just noticed that the functions should actually bound with GDVIRTUAL_BIND(), but I am not exactly sure.
author: Geometror

body: You're right, for FastNoiseLite they could be `const`. But then the interface methods would need to be `const` too and I am not sure if we should change it (since someone might want to add a noise generator which uses some cache structure internally or something else which requires write access in the get_noise/get_image functions). Or should we prohibit that as it might lead to bad library/API design?
author: Geometror

body:
It sounds like bad design to me. I care about this in particular because I use one of these in a multithreaded generator, and seeing a non-const call to a noise class from multiple threads is a bit worrying^^"
If someone reaaaally wants to mutate something they can use `mutable`, though for Noise algorithms specifically I don't see a good reason (or it's not noise anymore).
author: Zylann

body: Why isn't it just called `gradient`?
author: aaronfranke

body: I dont know if that's related since I didnt implement this PR, but when speaking of noise, "gradient" can also relate to the derivative of noise (which in practical terms gives normals, or displacement vectors instead of a single value). This property is for the very specific use case of generating a colored image with a fixed conversion formula used exclusively in the `get_image` function. Maybe it could be called `color_gradient`?
author: Zylann

body:
Ramp is the term used in industry standard VFX tools like Adobe AfterFX and Houdini, even Blender. 

Gradient is a type of noise. 
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gradient_noise 

Godot is the oddball by calling color ramps gradients.
author: TokisanGames

body: Interesting. Godot 2 used the name ColorRamp, but in Godot 3 it was renamed to Gradient. If it goes against the rest of the industry then that seems like a strange change, and I can see how it's confusing in the context of Gradient noise, but without this context it seems like a good rename (Gradient sounds better to me).
author: aaronfranke

body: As Zylann suggested, I think `color_gradient` (or in this specific case `modulation_color_gradient`) would be fine, since it sets it apart enaugh from a gradient in the context of noise generation.

<details><summary> Bad suggestion, nothing to see here :sweat_smile: </summary>
I believe the idea is right, however with a further look at the parameters of `_parse_expression`, it looks like the `true` here isn't completely random and was probably intended for `p_initializer`, which is used for ignoring locals. See:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/2f4d76f068b29783bde653406b51909b29a082a3/modules/gdscript/gdscript_compiler.cpp#L1988

So I believe it should be:

```suggestion
				GDScriptCodeGenerator::Address src_addr = _parse_expression(codegen, r_error, parameter->default_value, false, true);
```
</details>
author: cdemirer

body:
I think my earlier suggestion would actually break this:
```gdscript
func f(a, b=a): prints(a, b)
```
So just removing the `true` is probably the best (`false` is already the default).
```suggestion
				GDScriptCodeGenerator::Address src_addr = _parse_expression(codegen, r_error, parameter->default_value);
```

body:
I thought having `false` there explicitly might be clearer, but sure.

Is the ability to refer to "earlier" parameters intended? C++ does not allow that. If so, this should probably also be documented.
BTW, I think setting initializer to true would also break referring to other local variables.

When I get back to my machine, I will try to add some clarifying tests regarding that.

body: What does this change do?
author: akien-mga

body:
So how it worked was that you get two lists, just own properties and all the properties including parent classes. Both are reported in their bind order, similar to inspector. Then we iterate the list of all properties. The assumption is that the list of all properties contains all the own properties first. So by the time `EO` is null, the own properties have ended and it is safe to assume every other property is inherited.

While `EO` is true we compare the values (below) and move the cursor one position forward. This still works when I add sorting, but the caveat is that we can no longer assume that own properties are the first ones. They still follow the same order, just have gaps of inherited properties in between. So we can no longer use that old default value, but it's safe to default to `true` instead. Every own property will be caught by the check below, as long as there is anything left to check.

body:
I was having unexplained corruption with some texture dimensions and traced it to these returned width/height values (returned by `Image::get_image_mipmap_offset_and_dimensions`).

For example, on the 12x12 texture (block=4), I was seeing this. First loop iteration
```
{
  bw=12, bh=12
  s=144
  w = 6, h = 6
  *r_mm_width = (12>>1) = 6
  *r_mm_height = (12>>1) = 6
}
```
Second loop iteration
```
{
  bw=8, bh=8
  s=64
  w = 3, h = 3
  *r_mm_width = (8>>1) = 4 <-- wrong!
  *r_mm_height = (8>>1) = 4 <-- wrong!
}
```
Third loop iteration
```
{
  bw=4, bh=4
  s=16
  w = 1, h = 1
  *r_mm_width = (4>>1) = 2 <-- wrong!
  *r_mm_height = (4>>1) = 2 <-- wrong!
}
```

Note that the behavior of this function was altered slightly by 81ed9fa31bc63823235f64c88d441a8e530ab039
However, It was also wrong before. It's possible either that Godot always had this bug with 2x2 and 1x1 mipmaps reading out-of-bounds, or didn't cause an issue before because the old `etc` compression library ignored the out-of-bounds data.

I can't see any reason for the return values to use these "half-block" values. I would think if I want to know the dimensions of a mipmap level, I would want the actual dimensions, namely `w` and `h`. I can't find any callers of this code which would be adversely affected by this change.

In case you want to check my assertions, the only public functions using these `*r_mm_` return values are `Image::get_image_mipmap_size` and `Image::get_image_mipmap_offset_and_dimensions`.

I did some further digging into that logic, so a quick history breakdown. @reduz originally added this `*r_mm_width = bw;` statement in 0586e184490fd132f99acb1a67c788959cfdbade . This function was only used in one place, in what is now `servers/rendering/renderer_rd/renderer_storage_rd.cpp`. However, the function was only ever used with FORMAT_RGBA8 for the longest time, meaning block size is 1 and bw == w and bh == h. For this reason, I suspect reduz either never tested it with compressed formats, or had a different intended usecase in mind than what it has ultimately been used for.

Alright, I know I talk too much. I hope this answers your question about why this code change was necessary, why it was relevant to this PR, and why it evaded notice for so long. I'll also loop in @floppyhammer as they made the more recent commit here.

```suggestion
#ifndef _NO_DISCARD_

I agree about keeping the macro itself for now so that relevant changes are easy to cherry-pick to `3.x`.

body:
This message is worded with "may" as it's not possible to check whether the headers have been sent correctly – only whether cross-origin isolation is enabled (which itself depends on a secure context). The browser also does not inform us *why* cross-origin isolation is disabled, so we can only do guesswork here.

On the other hand, we can check whether the browser thinks the current page is a secure context (and that's what I did above to conditionally display that reason message).

In GDScript's implementation the `to_lower()` call is removed (since `camel_to_underscore(true)` already converts to lowercase) and a call to `strip_edges()` is added at the end which is equivalent to C#'s `Trim()`. So we should do that too for consistency.

```

body:
This description is true but it might be not descriptive enough as:

>relative to the origin of the [CanvasLayer] that this [CanvasItem] is in

doesn't state in what way it's relative to the origin (origin is just a point). It may be not obvious that the returned position is described in the given `CanvasLayer`'s basis vectors. Meaning if that `CanvasLayer` is anyhow rotated, scaled, skewed, etc. (in other words when its basis is not standard) then there still might be some confusion about the result.

A description like:

>Returns the mouse's position in the coordinate space of the [CanvasLayer] that this [CanvasItem] is in.

doesn't have this issue (at least I think so) but it's probably not user-friendly as there are many no-math users (_"what is coordinate space?!"_). Maybe such description would be good if "coordinate space" would link to some page describing what it means? :thinking: 

My point is that current descriptions (the same issue with the other similar changes) are not fully descriptive. There's still missing information (relative in what way?) in them. I can't find wording to be both descriptive ("in the coordinate space") and user-friendly ("relative to") in a compact way. Maybe you could come up with something like that.

body: A couple of lines down, a call to `mesh_surface_get_lod()` is called which accepts `distance * lod_distance_multiplier` which then divides some values by this to get effective screen size. `0.0` would cause the highest LOD to always be shown and `distance_min` would still take into account the distance between the camera and the mesh which shouldn't happen in an orthogonal camera. `1.0` would then just effectively pass in the `lod_distance_multiplier` itself which is the size of an orthogonal camera or the fov of a perspective camera thus removing any effect distance would have.

body:
Technically, we should be using the same formula as with shadow sizes above the default. However, this would result in too much peter-panning, so I went for a half-way compromise between not adjusting the bias at all and adjusting it to match the resolution perfectly.

The bias factors used for each resolution are:

- 256: `8.5`
- 512: `4.5`
- 1024: `2.5`
- 2048: `1.5`
- 4096: `1.0`
- 8192: `0.5`
- 16384: `0.25`

This applies to both directional and point light shadows.
author: Calinou

body:
This logic should be different for Point Light Shadows, as you have to have the resolution of the cell the shadow is using, not the entire shadow map.

This would incorrectly change bias for the same effective resolution of shadow if you go from a 4k shadow map with 4 4 4 4 quadrants and change to 8k shadow map resolution with 16 16 16 16 quadrants which effectively results in same resolution, just more shadows available.

body:
I know this is already merged but I decided to look over it anyway and I give it a ✅

Everything in this PR falls into one of these 3 categories:

* The float literal has an identical value to the double literal (such as sums of powers of two `0.5f` or integers `0.0f`, `1.0f`, etc)
* The value is something we don't need the exact value of (such as checking if a value is `> 0.9999f`)
* The value is in Color, which should be always 32-bit anyway (since more precision is useless for Color)

body:
There seems to be a inconsistency between the Gradle and SCons targets.

"Debug" template *are* built with `scons tools=no target=release_debug`. The `target=debug` platform is only used for actual engine debugging (i.e. getting dev asserts, more information on crashes, etc.), and we don't ship any binaries built with `target=debug` (this is only for custom builds for people who know they need it).

I see that `platform/android/detect.py` doesn't seem to do anything special for `target=release_debug` compared to `target=release`, which is a discrepancy with all other platforms (note that it's `startswith`, applies to both `release` and `release_debug`): https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/f5b9cbaff6f0a058d08187b3124948ae68848cd0/platform/android/detect.py#L189
author: akien-mga

body:
That doesn't match the build matrix for other platforms.

The official released (production) editor binaries use `target=release_debug`. The editor can't be used with `target=release` only, as debugging tools are then not included, and it would crash on errors instead of reporting them.

So the only two editor targets you should support are:
`tools=yes target=release_debug` -> production build, official releases
`tools=yes target=debug` -> development build, used by engine developers (slow and not meant for use in production)

```suggestion
    if os.access("bin/godot" + suffix, os.F_OK) and not os.access("bin/godot" + suffix, os.W_OK):
        suffixes = suffix.split(".")  # Split suffix string so we can add suffix number later
        extension = suffixes.pop()  # Store last suffix, should be the file extension.

        old_suffix = suffix  # Store old suffix to use in print statement
        suffix = ""

        MAXIMUM_ATTEMPTS = 10
        for suffix_number in range(1, MAXIMUM_ATTEMPTS + 1):
            new_suffix = ".".join(suffixes) + "." + str(suffix_number) + "." + extension
            if not os.access("bin/godot" + new_suffix, os.F_OK) or os.access(
                "bin/godot" + new_suffix, os.W_OK
            ):  # Check if this path can be written to
                suffix = new_suffix
                break

        if not suffix:
            print(
                "'bin/godot"
                + old_suffix
                + "' cannot be written to at this time. This can be caused when trying to compile to an executable that is currently open. Close Godot and try again."
            )
        else:
            print(
                "'bin/godot"
                + old_suffix
                + "' cannot be written to at this time. Compiling to 'bin/godot"
                + suffix
                + "' instead."
            )
    return suffix
```

body: The occlusion buffer size is not fixed, it depends on the "rendering/occlusion_culling/occlusion_rays_per_thread" project setting (I now see this may not be a very explicit setting name, we could change it). The resolution is chosen so the number of pixels in the occlusion buffer is approximately (rays_per_thread * cpu_physical_threads). This means CPUs with fewer cores will use a lower resolution, in an attempt to keep performance costs more even across devices.

All points must be placed on the same 2D plane, which means it is not possible to create arbitrary 3D shapes with a single [PolygonOccluder3D]. body:
It's hard to say for sure without digging around in Godbolt, but I think the compiler can optimize this more if we group together the `p_weight` multiplications:

```suggestion
						(2.0f * p_pre - 5.0f * p_from + 4.0f * p_to - p_post) * (p_weight * p_weight) +
						(-p_pre + 3.0f * p_from - 3.0f * p_to + p_post) * (p_weight * p_weight * p_weight));
```

An alternative implementation idea would be to wrap each step further down in parenthesis such that each step only uses one additional multiplication by `p_weight`. I'm not sure which is faster.

body:
No, this is not a character count, but a glyph count, e.g., depending on font `Ē` might be two glyphs `E` and macron, and `ffi` might be one ligature glyph.

`main->lines[i]` have a `char_offset` and `char_count` fields you can use.
You can get a range of characters on each auto-wrapped line (relative to the line) using:
```c++
Vector2i range = TS->shaped_text_get_range(main->lines[i].text_buf->get_line_rid(j));
```
author: bruvzg
